Ayahs

وَٱلطُّورِ (١)
By the Mount [Sinai], (1)
وَكِتَـٰبٍ مَّسْطُورٍ (٢)
by the Book inscribed (2)
فِى رَقٍّ مَّنشُورٍ (٣)
on an unrolled parchment; (3)
وَٱلْبَيْتِ ٱلْمَعْمُورِ (٤)
by the House greatly frequented; (4)
وَٱلسَّقْفِ ٱلْمَرْفُوعِ (٥)
by the vault raised high, (5)
وَٱلْبَحْرِ ٱلْمَسْجُورِ (٦)
by the surging sea: (6)
إِنَّ عَذَابَ رَبِّكَ لَوَٰقِعٌ (٧)
indeed your Lord’s punishment will surely befall. (7)
مَّا لَهُۥ مِن دَافِعٍ (٨)
There is none who can avert it. (8)

Content of Surah at-Tur

This Surah is also among those where the weight of its discussions rests upon the issue of the Resurrection and the fate of the righteous and the pure on one hand, and the wicked and the criminals on that Great Day on the other; although other topics regarding various ideological fields are also observed within it.

Altogether, the content of this Surah can be divided into six sections:

  1. The opening verses of the Surah, which begin with successive oaths, discuss Divine punishment, the signs of the Day of Judgment, the fire of Hell, and the retribution of the disbelievers (Verses 1 to 16).

  2. Another section of this Surah details the heavenly blessings and Divine favors in the Hereafter that await the God-fearing, drawing attention to them one after another; in fact, most heavenly blessings are mentioned in this part of the Surah (Verses 17 to 28).

  3. In another section, it speaks of the Prophethood of the Prophet (peace be upon him and his family), enumerating the accusations the enemies leveled against him and providing concise answers to them (Verses 29 to 34).

  4. The fourth section discusses Monotheism (Tawhid) and pursues this matter with clear reasoning (Verses 35 to 43).

  5. In another part of the Surah, it returns to the issue of the Resurrection and some characteristics of the Day of Judgment (Verses 44 to 47).

  6. Finally, in the last section of the Surah, which consists of only two verses, it concludes the previous discussions with instructions to the Noble Prophet of Islam (peace be upon him and his family) regarding patience, steadfastness, and the glorification and praise of the Lord, along with the promise of Divine support. Thus, it forms a cohesive, captivating logical and emotional collection that conquers the hearts of its listeners.

Additionally, the naming of this Surah as "at-Tur" is due to its first verse.

The Virtue of Reciting this Surah

It is mentioned in a Hadith that the Prophet (peace be upon him and his family) said:

"من قرأ سورة الطور كان حقا على اللَّه ان يؤمنه من عذابه و ان ينعمه فى جنته"

"Whoever recites Surah at-Tur, it is incumbent upon Allah to secure him from His punishment and to grant him bounty in His Paradise." [1] In another Hadith from Imam al-Baqir عليه السلام, we read:

"من قرء سورة الطور جمع اللَّه له خير الدنيا و الآخرة"

"Whoever recites Surah at-Tur, Allah gathers for him the good of this world and the Hereafter." [2] It is clear that all this immense reward in this world and the Hereafter belongs to those who make this "recitation" a means for "reflection," and that reflection, in turn, a means toward "action."

[^1] and [^2]: Majma' al-Bayan, Vol. 9, p. 162; Tafsir al-Burhan, Vol. 4, p. 240.

بِسْمِ اللَّهِ الرَّحْمنِ الرَّحِيمِ

وَ الطُّورِ (1) وَ كِتابٍ مَسْطُورٍ (2) فِي رَقٍّ مَنْشُورٍ (3) وَ الْبَيْتِ الْمَعْمُورِ (4)

وَ السَّقْفِ الْمَرْفُوعِ (5) وَ الْبَحْرِ الْمَسْجُورِ (6) إِنَّ عَذابَ رَبِّكَ لَواقِعٌ (7) ما لَهُ مِنْ دافِعٍ (8)

Commentary

By the Swelling Ocean!

This Surah is another of those that begin with oaths—oaths intended to state an important reality, namely the issue of the Day of Judgment, the Resurrection, and the accounting of human deeds.

The importance of this matter is such that Allah, in various verses of the Quran, has sworn by many sacred things to clarify the greatness of that Day and its certain occurrence.

The five oaths seen at the beginning of this Surah carry profound and thought-provoking meanings, which commentators have explored extensively.

He says: "By the Mount of Tur" (وَ الطُّورِ).

"And by a Book inscribed..." (وَ كِتابٍ مَسْطُورٍ).

"On fine parchment unrolled" (فِي رَقٍّ مَنْشُورٍ).

"And by the Much-Frequented House" (وَ الْبَيْتِ الْمَعْمُورِ).

"And the canopy raised high" (وَ السَّقْفِ الْمَرْفُوعِ).

"And by the ocean filled and swelling!" (وَ الْبَحْرِ الْمَسْجُورِ).

"Indeed, the punishment of your Lord will surely come to pass" (إِنَّ عَذابَ رَبِّكَ لَواقِعٌ).

"There is none to avert it" (ما لَهُ مِنْ دافِعٍ).

"Tur" literally means "mountain," but considering that this word appears in 10 verses of the Holy Quran—9 of which refer to "Mount Sinai," the mountain where revelation descended upon Moses—it becomes clear that in the verse under discussion (especially given the definite article 'al'), it carries the same meaning here.

Therefore, in the first stage, Allah has sworn by one of the sacred places on earth where Divine revelation descended.

Regarding the interpretation of "the inscribed Book" (كتاب مسطور), various possibilities have been suggested:

Some consider it a reference to the Preserved Tablet (al-Lawh al-Mahfuz), some to the Holy Quran, some to the record of deeds, and some to the Torah revealed to Moses.

However, in harmony with the oath preceding it, this expression refers either to the Torah or to all Heavenly Books.

The word "Raqq" (رَقّ) from the root "Riqqat" (رقت) originally means thin and delicate, and it is also applied to the paper or thin skin upon which something is written; and "Manshur" (مَنْشُور) means spread out or unrolled. (Some believe this word also encompasses the meaning of radiance and brilliance).

Thus, the oath is taken by a Book written on the finest of pages, which is simultaneously open and spread out, not folded or hidden! Regarding "al-Bayt al-Ma'mur" (the Much-Frequented House), various interpretations have also been given. Some consider it a reference to a house in the heavens directly above the Kaaba, which is "frequented" and inhabited by the worship of angels. This meaning is found in numerous narrations in various Islamic sources. [1] According to one narration, seventy thousand angels visit it every day and never return to it again.

Some have interpreted it as the "Kaaba" and the House of Allah on earth, which is always frequented and inhabited by pilgrims and visitors; and we know it is the first house built and inhabited for worship on earth.

Some have also said it refers to the heart of the believer, which is inhabited by faith and the remembrance of Allah.

However, the apparent meaning of the verse is one of the first two, and considering the various expressions in the Quran where the "Kaaba" is referred to as "the House" (al-Bayt), the second meaning seems most appropriate.

As for "the canopy raised high" (السقف المرفوع), it refers to the sky, as we read in Surah al-Anbiya, Verse 32: "And We made the sky a protected ceiling" (وَ جَعَلْنَا السَّماءَ سَقْفاً مَحْفُوظاً). And in Surah an-Nazi'at, Verses 27 and 28: "Are you a more difficult creation or is the heaven? He constructed it. He raised its ceiling and proportioned it."

The expression "ceiling" or "canopy" may be because the stars and celestial bodies cover the entire sky like a ceiling, or it may refer to the "atmosphere around the earth," where a dense layer of air surrounds it like a firm ceiling, protecting it well against the onslaught of meteors and harmful cosmic rays.

For "Masjur" (مَسْجُور), two meanings are mentioned in linguistics: one is "ablaze/kindled" and the other is "filled." Raghib in al-Mufradat says: "Sajr" (rhyming with Fajr) means to kindle a fire, and he considers the above verse to hold this meaning; he does not mention the second meaning. However, the late Tabarsi in Majma' al-Bayan mentions the first meaning as such, and it is also noted in some lexicons.

Other verses of the Quran also support the first meaning, as we read in Surah al-Mu'min, Verses 71 and 72: "They will be dragged into the scalding water; then into the Fire they will be kindled" (يُسْحَبُونَ فِي الْحَمِيمِ ثُمَّ فِي النَّارِ يُسْجَرُونَ).

In the words of the Commander of the Faithful, Ali عليه السلام, in the story of the "heated iron" to his brother Aqeel, we also read:

"أ تان من حديدة احماها انسانها للعبه و تجرنى الى نار سجرها جبارها لغضبه"

"Do you moan because of a piece of iron which a man has heated for his play, while you pull me toward a fire which its Compeller has kindled out of His wrath?" [1] But where is this "Bahr al-Masjur" (the kindled/swelling ocean)? Some have said it refers to these very oceans of our planet earth, which on the threshold of the Resurrection will be set ablaze and then explode, as stated in Surah at-Takwir, Verse 6: "And when the seas are set ablaze" (وَ إِذَا الْبِحارُ سُجِّرَتْ), and in Surah al-Infitar, Verse 3: "And when the seas are erupted" (وَ إِذَا الْبِحارُ فُجِّرَتْ).

However, others have interpreted it as the sea of molten material within the core of the earth. A narration cited in Tafsir al-Ayyashi from Imam al-Baqir عليه السلام also serves as evidence for this meaning; it states that "Qarun" (Korah) is punished in the "Bahr al-Masjur," [2] while we know the Holy Quran says: "So We caused the earth to swallow him and his home" (فَخَسَفْنا بِهِ وَ بِدارِهِ الْأَرْضَ - Qasas 81).

These two interpretations are not contradictory, and it is possible the verse swears by both, as both are among the signs of Allah and the great wonders of this world.

It is noteworthy that commentators have not discussed much the conceptual connection between these five oaths. However, it seems the first three oaths are closely related, as they all speak of revelation and its characteristics: "Mount Tur" was the site of revelation, "the inscribed Book" refers to the Heavenly Book (whether the Torah or the Quran), and "the Much-Frequented House" is the place of the coming and going of angels and the messenger of Divine revelation.

The other two oaths speak of "cosmic" signs (in contrast to the first three which spoke of "legislative" signs). These two oaths refer to the most important sign of Monotheism—the magnificent sky—and to one of the important signs of the Resurrection that occurs at the threshold of the Day of Judgment.

Therefore, "Monotheism," "Prophethood," and "Resurrection" are gathered in these five oaths.

Some who consider all these verses to refer to Moses and his story have linked the verses as follows: Tur is the mountain where revelation descended to Moses; the inscribed Book is the Torah; the Much-Frequented House is the center of the angel of revelation's movement (possibly referring to Bayt al-Maqdis); and the raised canopy is what is mentioned in the story of the Children of Israel: "And [remember] when We raised the mountain over them as if it were a canopy" (وَ إِذْ نَتَقْنَا الْجَبَلَ فَوْقَهُمْ كَأَنَّهُ ظُلَّةٌ - A'raf 171).

And the "Bahr al-Masjur" is the fiery sea in which Qarun is punished for opposing the religion of Moses.

However, this interpretation seems unlikely and is inconsistent with the narrations cited in Islamic sources. As we have said, "the raised canopy," by the testimony of other Quranic verses and narrations cited in the commentary of the verse, refers to the sky.

The point that remains is: what is the connection between these oaths and the subject for which the oath is taken?

The answer to this question becomes clear in light of what was mentioned above: these oaths, which revolve around the power of Allah in the realms of "creation" and "legislation," demonstrate that such a One is perfectly capable of returning the dead to life and establishing the Resurrection. This is exactly what the oaths are taken for, as we read in the final verses: "Indeed, the punishment of your Lord will surely come to pass; there is none to avert it."

[^1]: Bihar al-Anwar, more than ten narrations are cited in this regard (Vol. 58, p. 55 onwards). [^1]: Nahj al-Balagha, Sermon 224. [^2]: Nur al-Thaqalayn, Vol. 5, p. 138.

Ayahs

يَوْمَ تَمُورُ ٱلسَّمَآءُ مَوْرًا (٩)
On the day when the sky whirls violently, (9)
وَتَسِيرُ ٱلْجِبَالُ سَيْرًا (١٠)
and the mountains move with an awful motion: (10)
فَوَيْلٌ يَوْمَئِذٍ لِّلْمُكَذِّبِينَ (١١)
woe to the deniers on that day (11)
ٱلَّذِينَ هُمْ فِى خَوْضٍ يَلْعَبُونَ (١٢)
—those who play around in vain talk, (12)
يَوْمَ يُدَعُّونَ إِلَىٰ نَارِ جَهَنَّمَ دَعًّا (١٣)
the day when they will be shoved forcibly toward the fire of hell, [and told:] (13)
هَـٰذِهِ ٱلنَّارُ ٱلَّتِى كُنتُم بِهَا تُكَذِّبُونَ (١٤)
‘This is the Fire which you used to deny! (14)
أَفَسِحْرٌ هَـٰذَآ أَمْ أَنتُمْ لَا تُبْصِرُونَ (١٥)
Is this, then, [also] magic, or is it you who do not perceive? (15)
ٱصْلَوْهَا فَٱصْبِرُوٓا۟ أَوْ لَا تَصْبِرُوا۟ سَوَآءٌ عَلَيْكُمْ ۖ إِنَّمَا تُجْزَوْنَ مَا كُنتُمْ تَعْمَلُونَ (١٦)
Enter it, and it will be the same for you whether you are patient or impatient. You are only being requited for what you used to do.’ (16)

Commentary

Your Recompense is Naught but Your Own Deeds

In the preceding verses, a brief allusion was made to the Divine punishment on the Day of Resurrection. The verses under discussion provide an explanation and commentary on this meaning. First, they recount some of the characteristics of the Day of Resurrection, and then the nature of the punishment for the deniers.

It says: "This Divine punishment is on the day when the heaven (the celestial bodies) will be in a state of violent agitation, moving to and fro" (يَوْمَ تَمُورُ السَّماءُ مَوْراً) [1]. The word mawr (on the pattern of qawl) has been assigned various meanings in linguistics. Raghib says in Al-Mufradat: "Mawr means a rapid flow." He also states that the dust which the wind carries in every direction is called mawr.

In Lisan al-Arab, it is also mentioned that mawr means movement and coming and going; it has also been interpreted as waves and speed. Some have interpreted mawr as rotational or circular motion.

From the sum of these interpretations, it can be understood that mawr is that rapid, rotational movement accompanied by coming and going, agitation, and fluctuation.

In this way, on the threshold of the Resurrection, the governing system of the celestial bodies will be disrupted.

[1]: "Yawm" (Day) is in the accusative case as an adverb of time (zarf) and is connected to "waqi'" (befalling) mentioned in the previous verses.

They will deviate from their orbits and move in every direction, then they will be rolled up, and in their place, a new heaven will be established by the command of Allah, as verse 104 of Surah Al-Anbiya says: "يَوْمَ نَطْوِي السَّماءَ كَطَيِّ السِّجِلِّ لِلْكُتُبِ" ("The Day when We shall roll up the heavens like a scroll rolled up for books").

And in verse 48 of Surah Ibrahim, we read: "يَوْمَ تُبَدَّلُ الْأَرْضُ غَيْرَ الْأَرْضِ وَ السَّماواتُ" ("The Day when the earth will be changed to another earth and so will be the heavens").

In other verses of the Quran, expressions are seen that speak of the splitting of the celestial bodies (Al-Infitar: 1), their being stripped away (At-Takwir: 11), and the gaps appearing between them (Al-Mursalat: 9), which, God willing, we will discuss under those verses.

Then He adds: "And the day when the mountains will move with a [terrible] motion" (وَ تَسِيرُ الْجِبالُ سَيْراً). Yes, the mountains will be uprooted and set in motion, and then, according to the testimony of other Quranic verses, they will be disintegrated and become like "carded wool" (Al-Qari'ah: 5), and in their place, a flat, level earth, devoid of water and vegetation, will appear: "فَيَذَرُها قاعاً صَفْصَفاً" (Ta-Ha: 106) [1]. All of these are indications that this world and all its refuges will be crushed; a new world with new systems will take its place, and man will stand before the results of his own deeds.

[1]: For further explanation, refer to Volume 13 of Tafsir Nemouneh, page 302 (under verse 105 of Surah Ta-Ha).

Therefore, in the next verse, He adds: "Since it is so, woe on that Day to the deniers!" (فَوَيْلٌ يَوْمَئِذٍ لِلْمُكَذِّبِينَ) [1]. Indeed, while the terror and agitation resulting from the transformation of the world have gripped everyone, a greater terror comes to the "deniers," which is the Divine punishment, for "wayl" is an expression of regret and sorrow over the occurrence of an undesirable event.

Then, identifying these "deniers," He says: "Those who are occupied with playing in vain discourse" (الَّذِينَ هُمْ فِي خَوْضٍ يَلْعَبُونَ). They call the verses of the Quran "lies," the miracles of the Prophet (صلى الله عليه وآله) "magic," and they consider the bringer of it "insane." They take all truths as a game and face them with mockery and derision.

With vain and illogical speech, they rise to war against the truth, and to reach their goal, they do not hesitate to use any slander or lie. Khawd (on the pattern of hawd) means entering into vain speech, and originally it means entering into water and passing through it.

[1]: The "fa" in "fa-wayl" is a fa of consequence (tafri'), meaning: since there is no refuge on that day, woe to these deniers.

Once again, to introduce that Day and state the fate of these deniers, He provides another explanation, adding: "The Day they shall be pushed into the fire of Hell with a violent thrust" (يَوْمَ يُدَعُّونَ إِلى‌ نارِ جَهَنَّمَ دَعًّا) [1].

"And it will be said to them: This is the Fire which you used to deny!" (هذِهِ النَّارُ الَّتِي كُنْتُمْ بِها تُكَذِّبُونَ).

And it will also be said to them: "Is this then magic? Or do you not see?" (أَ فَسِحْرٌ هذا أَمْ أَنْتُمْ لا تُبْصِرُونَ).

In the world, you constantly said that what Muhammad (صلى الله عليه وآله) brought was magic; that through sorcery he had cast a veil over our eyes so that we would not see the truths; that he steals our intellect and presents matters to us as "miracles" and recites words to us as "Divine revelation," but all of these are baseless and nothing but magic.

Therefore, on the Day of Resurrection, as a rebuke and reproach, when they see the fire of Hell with their own eyes and feel its heat, it is said to them: "Is this magic? Has a veil been cast over your eyes?"

They are also told: "Enter it and burn; whether you are patient or impatient, it is all the same for you!" (اصْلَوْها فَاصْبِرُوا أَوْ لا تَصْبِرُوا سَواءٌ عَلَيْكُمْ).

"For your recompense is only your own deeds" (إِنَّما تُجْزَوْنَ ما كُنْتُمْ تَعْمَلُونَ).

Yes, it is your own deeds that have returned to you and entangled you; therefore, grieving, lamenting, sighing, and restlessness have no effect.

This verse is a renewed emphasis on the issue of the "embodiment of deeds" and their return to man, and also a renewed emphasis on the issue of the Lord's justice, for however scorching the fire of Hell may be and however painful its punishment, it is nothing but the result of the deeds of the humans themselves and their transformed forms.

[1]: Da'’ (on the pattern of jadd) means a violent push and driving away with harshness and force. "Yawm" is in the accusative as an adverb, or it is a substitute (badal) for "yawma'idhin" in the previous verse.

Key Points

1- How are the criminals taken to Hell?

Undoubtedly, taking them toward the fire of Hell is accompanied by humiliation, abasement, torment, and punishment. However, in various verses of the Quran, different expressions are seen in this regard:

In Surah Al-Haqqah, verses 30 and 31, we read: "خُذُوهُ فَغلُّوهُ ثُمَّ الْجَحِيمَ صَلُّوهُ" ("Seize him and shackle him, then cast him into the Blazing Fire").

And in Surah Ad-Dukhan, verse 47, it says: "خُذُوهُ فَاعْتِلُوهُ إِلى‌ سَواءِ الْجَحِيمِ" ("Seize him and drag him into the midst of Hell!"). And in several verses, the term sawq (driving) is used, such as verse 86 of Surah Maryam: "وَ نَسُوقُ الْمُجْرِمِينَ إِلى‌ جَهَنَّمَ وِرْداً" ("And We shall drive the criminals to Hell [like thirsty camels driven] to water").

Conversely, the pious and God-fearing are taken toward Paradise with the utmost respect and honor. The angels of Allah hasten to welcome them, the gates of Paradise are opened to them, and the keepers of Paradise greet them with peace and welcome, giving them the glad tidings of eternal residence in Paradise (Az-Zumar: 73).

In this way, not only are "Paradise" and "Hell" the centers of Allah's "mercy" and "wrath," but the formalities of entry into each of them also reflect this meaning.

2- Those who are immersed in vanities

Although the focus of the Quran in the above verses is on the polytheists of the Prophet's era, these verses undoubtedly have a general application and include all deniers—even materialist philosophers who are immersed in a handful of imaginations and incomplete thoughts. They treat the realities of existence as a game and recognize nothing except what they have perceived with their deficient intellects. They wait to see everything in their laboratories and under microscopes—even the Pure Essence of Allah! Otherwise, they do not recognize His existence.

These people are also instances of "فِي خَوْضٍ يَلْعَبُونَ" (immersed in vain play) and are drowned in a mass of false imaginations and fancies.

The human intellect, with all the brilliance it possesses, is like a candle before the world-illuminating sun when compared to the light of revelation. This candle allows him to emerge from the dark environment of the material world and open the door to the metaphysical world; then, in the sunlight of revelation, he must fly in every direction to see and know the infinite universe.

Ayahs

إِنَّ ٱلْمُتَّقِينَ فِى جَنَّـٰتٍ وَنَعِيمٍ (١٧)
Indeed the Godwary will be amid gardens and bliss, (17)
فَـٰكِهِينَ بِمَآ ءَاتَىٰهُمْ رَبُّهُمْ وَوَقَىٰهُمْ رَبُّهُمْ عَذَابَ ٱلْجَحِيمِ (١٨)
rejoicing because of what their Lord has given them, and that their Lord has saved them from the punishment of hell. (18)
كُلُوا۟ وَٱشْرَبُوا۟ هَنِيٓـًٔۢا بِمَا كُنتُمْ تَعْمَلُونَ (١٩)
[They will be told:] ‘Enjoy your food and drink, [as a reward] for what you used to do.’ (19)
مُتَّكِـِٔينَ عَلَىٰ سُرُرٍ مَّصْفُوفَةٍ ۖ وَزَوَّجْنَـٰهُم بِحُورٍ عِينٍ (٢٠)
They will be reclining on arrayed couches, and We will wed them to big-eyed houris. (20)
وَٱلَّذِينَ ءَامَنُوا۟ وَٱتَّبَعَتْهُمْ ذُرِّيَّتُهُم بِإِيمَـٰنٍ أَلْحَقْنَا بِهِمْ ذُرِّيَّتَهُمْ وَمَآ أَلَتْنَـٰهُم مِّنْ عَمَلِهِم مِّن شَىْءٍ ۚ كُلُّ ٱمْرِئٍۭ بِمَا كَسَبَ رَهِينٌ (٢١)
The faithful and their descendants who followed them in faith—We will make their descendants join them, and We will not stint anything from [the reward of] their deeds. Every person is hostage to what he has earned. (21)

Commentary

Everyone is Held in Pledge for Their Own Deeds

Following the discussions in the preceding verses regarding the punishments of the criminals and their painful torments, the verses under discussion point to their opposite: the abundant blessings and boundless rewards of the believers and the God-fearing, so that through a clear comparison, the position of each becomes more evident.

First, It says: "Indeed, the righteous will be in gardens and bliss" (إِنَّ الْمُتَّقِينَ فِي جَنَّاتٍ وَ نَعِيمٍ).

The use of the term "the righteous" (al-muttaqin) instead of "the believers" (al-mu'minin) is because this title encompasses both faith and aspects of righteous action; especially since "Taqwa" (piety/God-wariness) is, in one stage, the preamble and foundation of faith, as the Quran states in verse 2 of Surah Al-Baqarah: ذلِكَ الْكِتابُ لا رَيْبَ فِيهِ هُدىً لِلْمُتَّقِينَ: "This is the Book about which there is no doubt, a guidance for the righteous."

This is because if a human being does not possess commitment, a sense of responsibility, and a spirit of seeking and demanding the truth—which is a stage of Taqwa—he will never pursue the investigation of the true religion, nor will he accept the guidance of the Quran.

The use of "Gardens" (jannat) and "Bliss" (na'im) in the plural form, and as indefinite nouns, points to the diversity and greatness of those gardens and blessings.

Then, referring to the effect of these great blessings on the morale of the inhabitants of Paradise, It says: "They will be happy and joyful with what their Lord has given them, and they will speak sweet and pleasant words in this regard" (فاكِهِينَ بِما آتاهُمْ رَبُّهُمْ) [^1]. Indeed, they are overflowing with joy, constantly jesting with one another; their hearts are void of any grief or sorrow, and they feel an extraordinary tranquility.

Especially since: Allah has given them peace of mind regarding punishment: "and their Lord protected them from the punishment of Hellfire" (وَ وَقاهُمْ رَبُّهُمْ عَذابَ الْجَحِيمِ).

This sentence can have two meanings: first, it expresses an independent blessing alongside the other blessings of the Lord; second, it could be a continuation of the previous statement, meaning the inhabitants of Paradise are joyful for two things: first, for the blessings Allah has bestowed upon them, and second, for the punishments He has kept away from them.

Furthermore, the expression "their Lord" (rabbuhum) in both sentences is an allusion to Allah's ultimate grace toward them and the continuation of His Lordship (Rububiyyah) in that world.

After this brief and general reference to the blessings and the joy and happiness of the righteous in Paradise, It proceeds to detail them, saying: "It will be said to them: Eat and drink with enjoyment!" (كُلُوا وَ اشْرَبُوا هَنِيئاً).

[^1]: "Fakihin" is derived from the root "fakah" (on the pattern of 'nazar') and "fakahah" (on the pattern of 'shabahah'), meaning to be joyful and laughing, and to make others happy with sweet words and jesting. Al-Raghib says in Al-Mufradat: "Fakihah" means every kind of fruit, and "fakahat" refers to the conversations of intimate companions. Some have suggested that in the above verse, the phrase "فاكِهِينَ بِما آتاهُمْ رَبُّهُمْ" refers to consuming various fruits, but this meaning seems unlikely.

"This is because of the deeds you used to do" (بِما كُنْتُمْ تَعْمَلُونَ).

The expression "hani'an" (with enjoyment/wholesome) indicates that the food and drink of Paradise do not result in any undesirable side effects; they are not like the blessings of this world, where sometimes a slight excess or deficiency leads to illness and discomfort.

Moreover, neither is hardship required to obtain them, nor is there any fear of them ending or being exhausted; for this reason, these blessings are completely wholesome [^1]. It is certain that the blessings of Paradise are inherently wholesome, but the fact that the angels say "may it be wholesome for you" to the inhabitants of Paradise is a grace and wholesomeness of its own.

Another blessing is that they "will be reclining on thrones lined up in rows" (مُتَّكِئِينَ عَلى‌ سُرُرٍ مَصْفُوفَةٍ). And they will benefit greatly from the pleasure of intimacy with friends and other believers, which is a spiritual pleasure superior to many other delights.

"Surur" is the plural of "sarir" (from the root 'surur' meaning joy). Originally, it refers to thrones or couches prepared for gatherings of intimacy and joy, upon which people recline.

"Masfufah" is from the root "saff," meaning that these thrones are placed next to one another, forming a great gathering of intimacy.

In several verses of the Quran, we read that the inhabitants of Paradise sit on thrones facing one another: "عَلى‌ سُرُرٍ مُتَقابِلِينَ" (Al-Hijr 47, As-Saffat 44).

This expression does not contradict what is mentioned in the verse under discussion, because in gatherings of intimacy and joy that are free from disparity and discrimination, chairs are placed side by side and around the gathering, which both forms a continuous row and allows them to face one another. The expression "reclining" (muttaki'in) points to their ultimate tranquility, for a person usually reclines when at peace, whereas those who are anxious and restless usually do not.

Then It adds: "And We will marry them to fair maidens with large, beautiful eyes" (وَ زَوَّجْناهُمْ بِحُورٍ عِينٍ) [^1].

These are part of the "material" and "spiritual" blessings of the inhabitants of Paradise, but It does not stop there; It adds another part of the spiritual and material favors: "And those who believed and whose offspring followed them in faith—We will join their offspring with them [in Paradise], and We will not deprive them of anything of their deeds" (وَ الَّذِينَ آمَنُوا وَ اتَّبَعَتْهُمْ ذُرِّيَّتُهُمْ بِإِيمانٍ أَلْحَقْنا بِهِمْ ذُرِّيَّتَهُمْ وَ ما أَلَتْناهُمْ مِنْ عَمَلِهِمْ مِنْ شَيْ‌ءٍ).

This is also a great blessing: that a person sees his faithful and beloved children beside him in Paradise and enjoys their company, without anything being diminished from his own deeds.

From the expressions in the verse, it appears that the reference is to adult children who walk in the path of their fathers, follow them in faith, and join them in terms of their school of thought.

If such individuals have shortcomings or negligences in their actions, Allah—out of respect for their righteous fathers—forgives them, elevates their status, and brings them to the rank of their fathers. This is a great favor for both fathers and children [^1]. However, a group of commentators has interpreted "offspring" (dhurriyyah) here in a general sense, such that it includes young children as well. But this interpretation does not align with the apparent meaning of the verse, as "following in faith" implies reaching the stage of maturity or near it.

Unless it is said that young children reach the stage of maturity on the Day of Resurrection and are tested; if they emerge successful from this test, they are joined to their fathers, as mentioned in a Hadith narrated in the book Al-Kafi. When Imam عليه السلام was asked about the children of believers, he replied: "When the Day of Resurrection comes, Allah gathers them, kindles a fire, and commands them to throw themselves into it. Those who carry out this command, the fire becomes cool and safe for them, and they are the successful ones; and those who refuse are deprived of Allah's grace" [^1]. However, this Hadith, besides being weak in its chain of narration, has other issues in its text that are not appropriate to explain here.

Of course, there is no obstacle to young children also entering Paradise out of respect for their fathers and being placed beside them; the question is whether the above verse refers to this matter or not. As we said, the expression "following the fathers in faith" indicates that adults are intended.

In any case, since the elevation of these children to the rank of their fathers might create the illusion that something is taken from the fathers' deeds and given to the children, it is followed by: "وَ ما أَلَتْناهُمْ مِنْ عَمَلِهِمْ مِنْ شَيْ‌ءٍ" (And We will not diminish anything from their deeds).

Ibn Abbas narrates from the Noble Prophet صلى الله عليه وآله that he said: "When a man enters Paradise, he asks about his parents, his wife, and his children. It is said to him: 'They did not reach your rank or your level of deeds.' He says: 'O Lord! I performed deeds for myself and for them.' Thereupon, it is commanded that they be joined with him" [^2] [^3]. It is noteworthy that at the end of the verse, It adds: "Every person is a pledge for what he has earned" (كُلُّ امْرِئٍ بِما كَسَبَ رَهِينٌ).

[^1]: "Hur" is the plural of "Hawra" and "Ahwar," referring to someone whose pupils are intensely black and the whites of the eyes are intensely transparent, or it is generally a metaphor for complete beauty, as beauty is most manifested in the eyes. "In" is the plural of "A'yan" and "Ayna," meaning large-eyed. Thus, the words "Hur" and "In" apply to both masculine and feminine and have a broad concept that includes all heavenly spouses: female spouses for believing men and male spouses for believing women (take note).

[^1]: It appears that the sentence "وَ الَّذِينَ آمَنُوا..." is an independent sentence, and the "waw" is for inception (istinaf). A group of commentators (such as Allamah Tabataba'i, Maraghi, and the author of Fi Zilal) have chosen this meaning. However, it is strange that Zamakhshari in Al-Kashshaf considers it coordinated (atf) with "Hur al-In," while it does not yield a suitable meaning that aligns with the eloquence and rhetoric of the Quran.

[^1]: Nur al-Thaqalayn, Vol. 5, p. 139 (summarized).

[^2]: Tafsir al-Maraghi, Vol. 27, p. 26.

[^3]: "Alatnahum" is from the root "alat" (on the pattern of 'shart'), meaning to diminish.

Therefore, it is not surprising that nothing is diminished from the deeds of the righteous and their rewards, because these deeds are with the person everywhere. If Allah shows grace and favor toward the children of the righteous and joins them with the righteous in Paradise, this does not mean that anything is diminished from the reward of their deeds.

Some commentators have taken "rahin" here to mean "hostage" or "pledge" in an absolute sense, believing the concept of the verse is that every human being is held in pledge for his own deeds, whether good or bad, and is rewarded or punished accordingly.

However, considering that this expression is not very suitable for good deeds, some other commentators have considered "every soul" here to refer only to evildoers, saying: every human being is a hostage for his own transgressions and polytheistic acts, and is in truth a prisoner and captive of them.

Sometimes they also argue using verses 38 and 39 of Surah Al-Muddaththir: كُلُّ نَفْسٍ بِما كَسَبَتْ رَهِينَةٌ إِلَّا أَصْحابَ الْيَمِينِ: "Every soul is a pledge for what it has earned, except the companions of the right" (those whose record of deeds is given to their right hand and are the people of salvation).

But this interpretation also does not seem appropriate, given that the preceding and following verses are all about the "righteous" and the God-fearing, and there is no mention of polytheism, polytheists, or criminals in them.

In contrast to these two interpretations, each of which is unsuitable in some respect, there is a third interpretation that is completely consistent with the beginning of the verse and the preceding and following verses: one of the meanings of "rahn" in the language is "accompaniment" and "being joined with something." Although the well-known meaning of "rahn" is "collateral" against a loan, it is understood from the words of linguists that one of its meanings is also "permanence" and "accompaniment" [^1]. In fact, some of them explicitly state its original meaning to be this permanence and fixity, and consider "rahn" in the sense of "collateral" to be among the technical terms of the jurists. Therefore, when "ni'mah rahinah" is said, it means a lasting and established blessing [^2]. The Commander of the Faithful, Ali عليه السلام, says regarding past nations: "ها هم رهائن القبور و مضامين اللحود": "Behold, they are the companions of the graves and those contained within the niches of the tombs" [^3]. Accordingly, the sentence "كُلُّ امْرِئٍ بِما كَسَبَ رَهِينٌ" means that the deeds of every person are his constant companion and will never be separated from him, whether the deed is good or bad. For this reason, the "righteous" are in Paradise with their deeds, and if their children are placed beside them, it does not mean that their deeds are diminished.

Regarding the verse in Surah Al-Muddaththir which excludes the "Companions of the Right" from this meaning, it may point to the fact that they are subject to graces that are beyond reckoning, such that their deeds have no significance in the face of those divine graces [^4]. In any case, this sentence is an emphasis on the reality that a person's deeds are never separated from him and are constantly with him in all stages and stations!

[^1]: Lisan al-Arab, root "rahn."

[^2]: Majma' al-Bahrayn, root "rahn."

[^3]: Nahj al-Balagha, Letter 45.

[^4]: Regarding the exception of the "Companions of the Right" in Surah Al-Muddaththir, there are other interpretations which, God willing, will be mentioned under that same verse.

But Allah showed us favour and He saved us from the punishment of the [infernal] miasma. (27)

We used to supplicate Him aforetime. Indeed He is the All-benign, the All-merciful.’ (28)

Commentary

That Day We Were Afraid, and Today We Are in Absolute Security

In the preceding verses, nine categories of the blessings bestowed upon the people of Paradise were mentioned. In the verses under discussion, five more categories are highlighted, such that from the collective whole, it is clearly understood that everything necessary for peace, comfort, pleasure, joy, and happiness is provided for them in Paradise.

First, referring to two types of food for the inhabitants of Paradise, He says: "And We will provide them with fruit and meat from whatever they desire" (وَ أَمْدَدْناهُمْ بِفاكِهَةٍ وَ لَحْمٍ مِمَّا يَشْتَهُونَ).

The word amdadnahum is derived from the root imdad, meaning to continue, increase, and bestow. This implies that the fruits and foods of Paradise are not such that they diminish by consumption, nor are they like the fruits of this world which fluctuate greatly with the seasons; rather, they are perpetual, eternal, and continuous.

The expression mimma yashtahun ("from whatever they desire") indicates that the people of Paradise are completely free in choosing the type, quantity, and quality of these fruits and foods; whatever they wish for is at their disposal.

Of course, the foods of Paradise are not limited to these two, but these are two significant types.

Placing "fruit" (fakiha) before "meat" (lahm) is an indication of the superiority of fruits over meats.

Then, referring to the delightful drinks of the people of Paradise, He adds: "There they will pass from one to another a cup [of pure wine] wherein there is no idle talk nor sin" (يَتَنازَعُونَ فِيها كَأْساً لا لَغْوٌ فِيها وَ لا تَأْثِيمٌ).

Rather, it is a drink that is wholesome, pleasurable, invigorating, and soul-nourishing, free from any intoxication or corruption of the intellect. Consequently, there is never any idle talk or sin following it; instead, it is entirely characterized by alertness and physical and spiritual pleasure.

The word yatanaza‘un is derived from tanazu‘, meaning to take from one another, and sometimes it carries the meaning of "pulling back and forth or contention." Therefore, some commentators have said: This sentence indicates that the people of Paradise, by way of jest, playfulness, and to increase joy and expansiveness, playfully pull the cups of "pure wine" from one another's hands and drink.

However, as some lexicographers have stated: whenever tanazu‘ is used regarding an object like a ka’s (cup), it means taking from one another, not conflict or tugging.

It is also noteworthy that ka’s refers to a cup that is full of wine; an empty vessel is not called a ka’s [1]. In any case, since the term ka’s might occasionally evoke the intoxicating wines of this world, He adds that in that wine there is neither laghw (idle talk) nor sin, because it never robs a person of their intellect or consciousness. Therefore, the incoherent speech and ugly actions that stem from the intoxicated will never proceed from them; rather, by virtue of the fact that the wine is

[1]: Al-Raghib says in Al-Mufradat: "The ka’s is the vessel along with the drink it contains." The same interpretation is given in Majma‘ al-Bahrayn. If it is empty of wine, it is called a qadah.

pure (tahur), it makes them purer, more sincere, and more alert.

Then, He addresses the fourth blessing, which is the blessing of the presence of heavenly servants, saying: "And there will circulate among them young boys for them, as if they were pearls well-guarded [in shells]" (وَ يَطُوفُ عَلَيْهِمْ غِلْمانٌ لَهُمْ كَأَنَّهُمْ لُؤْلُؤٌ مَكْنُونٌ).

A "pearl inside a shell" is so fresh, translucent, and beautiful that it knows no bounds. Although it retains much of its beauty outside the shell, the dust of the air and the contamination of hands, however slight, diminish its luster. The heavenly servants are so beautiful, fair-faced, and pure that it is as if they are pearls still in their shells! The expression yatufu ‘alayhim ("they circulate around them") indicates their constant readiness to serve.

Although there is no actual "need" for servants in Paradise, and whatever they desire is placed at their disposal, this in itself is a form of greater honor and respect for the people of Paradise.

In a hadith, it is narrated that the Prophet صلى الله عليه وآله was asked: "If the servant is like a pearl in a shell, what are the served—meaning the believers in Paradise—like?" He replied:

"By Him in whose hand is my soul, the superiority of the served over the servant there is like the superiority of the moon on the night of the full moon over the rest of the stars." [1] The expression lahum ("for them") shows that each of the believers has servants specific to themselves.

And since Paradise is not a place of grief or sorrow, those servants also derive the utmost pleasure from serving the believers.

The final blessing in this series is the blessing of complete peace and reassurance of heart regarding any form of punishment or retribution, as the next verse says: "And they will approach one another, inquiring [of each other]" about their past state, and they will enjoy comparing it with their state in Paradise (وَ أَقْبَلَ بَعْضُهُمْ عَلى‌ بَعْضٍ يَتَساءَلُونَ).

"They will say: 'Indeed, we were previously among our families fearful [of Allah]'" (قالُوا إِنَّا كُنَّا قَبْلُ فِي أَهْلِنا مُشْفِقِينَ). Even though we lived among our families, where one should feel secure, we were still fearful; we feared that unpleasant life events or divine punishment might arrive at any moment and overtake us.

We feared that our children and family might take the path of error and become lost and wandering in the valley of misguidance.

And we feared that cold-hearted enemies might take us by surprise and tighten the circle around us.

"But Allah has been gracious to us, and His vast mercy encompassed us, and He protected us from the punishment of the Scorching Wind" (فَمَنَّ اللَّهُ عَلَيْنا وَ وَقانا عَذابَ السَّمُومِ). Yes, the Merciful Lord rescued us from the prison of the world with all its terrors and settled us in the center of His blessings, which is Paradise.

When they recall their past and remember its details, and compare it with the state they are currently in, they realize the value of Allah's great blessings and His gifts even more, and naturally, it will be more pleasurable and delightful for them, because in this comparison, values are better clarified.

In the final words quoted from them here, the people of Paradise confess to the reality that they feel God's goodness and mercy there more than ever before. They say: "Indeed, we used to supplicate Him before; indeed, He is the Beneficent, the Merciful" (إِنَّا كُنَّا مِنْ قَبْلُ نَدْعُوهُ إِنَّهُ هُوَ الْبَرُّ الرَّحِيمُ). But here, we realize the reality and depth of these attributes more—how He has bestowed all this goodness in exchange for our insignificant deeds, and how He has included us in His mercy despite all those slips.

Yes, the scene of Resurrection and the blessings of Paradise are the manifestation of Allah's Names and Attributes, and the believers, by witnessing these scenes, become more acquainted with the truth of these Names and Attributes than at any other time. Even Hell expresses His attributes and demonstrates His wisdom, justice, and power.

Key Points

1- Yatasa’alun is from the root su’al, meaning to ask one another. It indicates that each of the people of Paradise asks their friends and inquires about their past state, because remembering these issues—the rescue from all those

pains and hardships and reaching all these blessings—is itself a pleasure. It is just as when a person returns from a dangerous journey and sits in a safe and secure environment, discussing the past with their spouse and expressing happiness over their rescue.

2- Mushfiqin is from the root ishfaq. As Al-Raghib says in Al-Mufradat, it means "attention mixed with fear." When it is made transitive with the word min, the concept of "fear" is more apparent, and when it is made transitive with the word fi, the concept of "attention and care" is more prominent. This word is originally derived from the root shafaq, which is the light mixed with darkness.

Now, we must see what they feared in the world and what they gave attention and care to.

There are three possibilities here, all of which we have combined in the commentary of the verse, as there is no contradiction between them: (fear of Allah and attention to one's own salvation; fear of the family's deviation and attention to their upbringing; and fear of enemies and attention to self-preservation against them). However, considering the subsequent verses, especially the phrase "But Allah has been gracious to us and protected us from the punishment of the Scorching Wind," the first meaning seems more appropriate.

3- The expression fi ahlina ("among our families"), which has a broad meaning here and includes all children, spouses, friends, and companions, indicates that a person normally

feels more secure among such a group than anywhere else. When one is fearful and apprehensive even among them, their state in other situations is obvious.

There is also the possibility that this expression refers to those who were trapped within a non-believing family and were even afraid of them, yet they resisted and, relying on divine grace, maintained their independence and did not conform to the crowd.

4- Samum refers to a heat that enters the masam of the body (the tiny pores on the surface of the skin) and harms or kills a person. The samum wind is such a wind, and the "punishment of samum" is such a punishment. The application of the word samm (poison) to lethal substances is also because of their penetration into the entire body.

5- Barr—as Al-Raghib says in Al-Mufradat—originally means "land" (as opposed to bahr, the sea). Subsequently, this term was applied to those whose good deeds are expansive and vast, and the most deserving of this name is the Pure Essence of Allah, whose goodness has encompassed all the inhabitants of the worlds.

6- Summary of the Verses

We said that in these verses and the preceding ones, fourteen categories of the blessings of the people of Paradise have actually been mentioned:

The gardens of Paradise (jannat)—various blessings (na‘im)—joy and happiness—security from the punishment of Hell—wholesome eating and drinking of heavenly foods and beverages—reclining on thrones lined up together—spouses from the hur al-‘ayn—the joining of

believing children with them—various types of delightful fruits—various types of meats—whatever they desire—cups full of pure wine—pearl-like servants—and finally, the formation of a gathering of intimacy, remembering the past, and enjoying the current state! Some of these blessings have a material aspect, while in others, the spiritual aspect is dominant. Nevertheless, the material and spiritual blessings of Paradise are not limited to these; rather, what has been mentioned are only glimpses of them.

Let them bring a discourse like it, if they are truthful. (34)

Occasion of Revelation

It is mentioned in a narration that the Quraysh gathered in "Dar al-Nadwa" [^1] to deliberate on how to prevent the call of the Prophet of Islam صلى الله عليه وآله, which was considered a great threat to their illegitimate interests.

One of the men from the tribe of "Bani Abd al-Dar" said: "We must wait for him to die, for he is ultimately a poet and will soon pass away, just as Zuhayr, Nabighah, and A'sha (three poets of the Pre-Islamic era) passed away (and their influence vanished; thus, Muhammad's صلى الله عليه وآله influence will also vanish with his death)." They said this and dispersed. The above verses were revealed and provided them with an answer ^2.

Commentary

If They Speak the Truth, Let Them Bring a Discourse Like It

In the preceding verses, significant portions regarding the blessings of Paradise and the rewards of the righteous were mentioned, and in the verses before those, a part of the painful punishments of the people of Hell was described.

[^1]: "Dar al-Nadwa" was the house of "Qusayy ibn Kilab," the famous ancestor of the Arabs, where they would gather for consultation on important matters. This house was located next to the House of Allah, and its door opened toward the "Kaaba." Its central role for consultative assemblies dated back to the time of Qusayy ibn Kilab himself (Sirah Ibn Hisham, Vol. 2, p. 124 and Vol. 1, p. 132).

^2: Tafsir al-Maraghi, Vol. 27, p. 31.

In the first verse under discussion, as a conclusion to the previous verses, He says: "Therefore, remind and exhort" (فذكر).

For the hearts of truth-seekers become more prepared upon hearing these words, and the time has come for you to express the words of truth to them.

This expression clearly shows that the primary goal of mentioning all those blessings and punishments for these two groups is to prepare the spiritual ground for accepting new truths. In fact, every speaker must utilize this method for their speech to be effective and for their words to have influence.

Then, addressing the accusations and false attributions that the stubborn and antagonistic enemies leveled against the Prophet صلى الله عليه وآله, He says: "By the grace of your Lord, and by the blessing of His favors, you are neither a soothsayer nor a madman!" (فَما أَنْتَ بِنِعْمَةِ رَبِّكَ بِكاهِنٍ وَ لا مَجْنُونٍ).

A "soothsayer" (Kahin) was someone who claimed to give news of the unseen secrets and often claimed to be in contact with the Jinn, receiving hidden news from them. Especially during the Age of Ignorance (Jahiliyyah), there were many soothsayers, including the two famous ones, "Shiqq" and "Satih." They were actually clever individuals who misused their intelligence and kept people preoccupied with these claims. Soothsaying (Kihanat) is forbidden and prohibited in Islam, and no credit is given to the words of soothsayers, because the secrets of the Unseen belong exclusively to Allah, and He then teaches whatever He deems appropriate to any of the Prophets and Imams علیهم السلام.

In any case, the Quraysh would hurl these accusations at the Prophet of Islam صلى الله عليه وآله to disperse people from around him. Sometimes they called him a soothsayer, and sometimes a madman; surprisingly, they were not even aware of the contradiction between these two descriptions, for soothsayers were clever individuals, unlike the madman. The combination of these two slanders in the above verse perhaps points to their incoherent rambling.

Then it addresses the third accusation, which also contradicts the previous attributes, saying: "Or do they say: 'He is a poet for whom we await some calamity of time (death)?'" (أَمْ يَقُولُونَ شاعِرٌ نَتَرَبَّصُ بِهِ رَيْبَ الْمَنُونِ). As long as he is alive, his poems flourish and attract people to him; wait a little until his death arrives, and the book of his poetry, like the scroll of his life, will be rolled up and placed in the niche of oblivion. On that day, we shall be at peace! As gathered from books of linguistics and exegesis, "Manun" is derived from the root "Mann," which originally has two meanings: "deficiency" and "cutting or severing," both of which are close in concept.

Subsequently, the word "Manun" was applied to death because "it diminishes the number and cuts off support" (ينقص العدد و يقطع المدد). Sometimes "Manun" is also used for the passage of time, as it also causes death, the severing of ties, and the reduction of numbers. Sometimes night and day are called "Manun," apparently for the same reason [^1]. As for the word "Rayb," it originally means doubt, uncertainty, and the suspicion of something that will later be unveiled, revealing its reality.

When this expression is used regarding death and "Rayb al-Manun" is said, it is from the perspective that the time of its occurrence is unknown, not the fact of its realization ^2. However, a group of commentators have interpreted "Rayb al-Manun" in the verse under discussion as

[^1]: Refer to "Lisan al-Arab," "Mufradat al-Raghib," "Al-Munjid," and "Tafsir al-Qurtubi."

"the calamities of time." It is even narrated from Ibn Abbas that the word "Rayb" everywhere in the Quran means "doubt and uncertainty" except in this verse of Surah At-Tur, where it means "calamities" [^1]. Some commentators have also interpreted it as a state of "anxiety and distress." Therefore, "Rayb al-Manun" is the state of anxiety that strikes most individuals before death.

It is possible that this interpretation returns to the aforementioned meaning, as the state of doubt and uncertainty is usually the source of anxiety and distress. Similarly, unforeseen events also bring a kind of anxiety, doubt, and uncertainty with them. Thus, all these concepts lead back to the root of "doubt and uncertainty" which is the original meaning of this word.

In other words, three meanings have been mentioned for "Rayb": doubt, anxiety, and calamities, and these are necessary concomitants of one another.

In any case, they comforted themselves with the hope that some events would occur and the scroll of the Prophet's صلى الله عليه وآله life would be rolled up, and they—in their own imagination—would be rid of this great problem that his call had created throughout their society. The Quran responds to these antagonistic, blind-hearted people with a meaningful and threatening sentence, saying: "Say: Wait! For I too am waiting with you!" (قُلْ تَرَبَّصُوا فَإِنِّي مَعَكُمْ مِنَ الْمُتَرَبِّصِينَ).

You wait for the realization of your crude fantasies, and I too am waiting for the divine punishment to befall you.

[^1]: "Qurtubi," Vol. 9, p. 6242.

You wait for the death of me so that the carpet of Islam may be rolled up, and I too, by the help of my Lord, wait for the religion of Islam to become world-encompassing during my life, and to continue its path after me, becoming universal and eternal. Yes, you rely on your imaginations and fantasies, while I rely on the special grace of my Lord.

Then He subjects them to the severest of rebukes, saying: "Do their minds command them to this? Or are they a transgressing people?" (أَمْ تَأْمُرُهُمْ أَحْلامُهُمْ بِهذا أَمْ هُمْ قَوْمٌ طاغُونَ) [^1]. The leaders of the Quraysh were known among their people as "Dhawi al-Ahlam" (possessors of intellect)! The Quran says: What kind of intellect is it that considers this heavenly revelation—whose signs of truthfulness are apparent from its entire content—to be poetry and soothsaying? And labels its bringer—who has a very long history of trustworthiness and wisdom—as a soothsayer, a madman, and a poet?! Therefore, it must be concluded that such accusations and slanders are not the command of their intellect, but rather the source of all of them is the spirit of rebellion and transgression that dominates these individuals. As soon as they see their illegitimate interests in danger, they bid farewell to intellect

[^1]: Regarding whether "Am" here is "interrogative" or "disjunctive" (meaning "Nay"), commentators have offered various possibilities, though they mostly prefer the second. However, the context of the verses fits the first meaning. But it should be noted that "Am" in such cases must follow an interrogative Hamza; hence, Fakhr al-Razi mentioned an implied phrase: "Has a reminder been sent down to them from Allah, or do their intellects command them to this?" (أ أنزل عليهم ذكر ام تامرهم احلامهم بهذا), pointing out that a human must either follow textual evidence or intellectual evidence.

and rise in rebellion against the command of Truth.

"Ahlam" is the plural of "Hilm" (with the vowel 'i'), meaning "intellect." However, according to "Raghib," "Hilm" actually means "self-restraint during the excitement of anger," which is considered one of the signs of intellect and sagacity (and shares a common root with 'Hilm' meaning forbearance).

This word (Hulm) is sometimes used to mean "dream and vision," and in the verse under discussion, such an interpretation is not far-fetched; meaning their words are as if they are the result of disturbed dreams.

Once again, referring to another of their accusations, which is actually the fourth in this series of charges, He adds: "Or do they say: 'He has forged it (this Quran)'? Nay, they do not believe!" (أَمْ يَقُولُونَ تَقَوَّلَهُ بَلْ لا يُؤْمِنُونَ).

"Taqawwul" (from the root 'Taqawwala') means a speech that a person fabricates by himself without it having any reality [^1]. This was another of the excuses of the polytheists and stubborn disbelievers for not submitting to the Glorious Quran and the Prophet's call, which has been repeatedly mentioned in the verses of the Quran.

But the Glorious Quran gives them a crushing response: It says, "Then let them bring a discourse like it, if they are truthful" (فَلْيَأْتُوا بِحَدِيثٍ مِثْلِهِ إِنْ كانُوا صادِقِينَ).

You too are human and, by your own claim, possess abundant intelligence, eloquence, and mastery over various forms of speech. Why are your speakers and thinkers unable to bring a discourse like it?! The phrase "Let them bring..." (فلياتوا) is what is termed a "command of incapacitation" (amr ta'jizi), and its goal is to clarify their helplessness and inability to produce the like of the Quran. This is what is referred to in the science of Theology (Kalam) and beliefs as "Tahaddi" (Challenge)—that is, inviting opponents to contest and produce the like of miracles.

In any case, this is one of the verses that clearly highlights the miraculous nature (I'jaz) of the Quran, and its concept is not limited to the contemporaries of the Prophet صلى الله عليه وآله. Rather, all those in all centuries and ages who say the Quran is human speech and has been forged against Allah are also the addressees of this call: if they are truthful, let them bring a discourse like it.

And as we know, this call of the Quran in this verse and similar verses has always been raised. Throughout the fourteen centuries that have passed since the mission of the Prophet صلى الله عليه وآله, no one has been able to respond to it positively, despite the fact that it is certain that the enemies of Islam—especially the masters of the Church and the Jews—spend billions annually on propaganda against Islam. What prevented them from placing a portion of that at the disposal of a group of opposing scholars, literati, and critics of speech so they could rise to challenge the Quran and be an instance of "let them bring a discourse like it"? This universal helplessness is a living witness to the authenticity of this heavenly revelation.

One of the commentators has a point here that is noteworthy; he says:

In this Quran, there is a special secret that everyone who encounters its verses feels, even before the secrets of its miraculous nature are discussed.

In the expressions of this Quran, one feels a special influence and authority, and something beyond these meanings is reflected in the human intellect. Within its expressions lies a hidden element that, upon mere listening, pours into the human being. Some perceive it more clearly, and some more subtly, but in any case, this authority and influence exist—a mysterious influence whose source cannot be easily identified.

Is it these words and expressions of the Quran that possess such attraction?

Or is there a secret hidden in the depth of its meanings?

Or is it the reflections that shine from its lights, or is it all of these?

Whatever it is, it is different from all other words and concepts cast into the mold of languages.

This is a secret hidden in the Quranic verses, which everyone perceives at the first encounter, and following that, they seek other secrets obtained through reflection and thought throughout the Quran [^1]. For more explanation regarding the miraculous nature of the Quran from various perspectives, refer to the first volume of Tafsir Nemouneh (under verse 23 of Surah Al-Baqarah), where a detailed discussion on this subject is provided, as well as in Volume 12 (under verse 88 of Surah Al-Isra).

[^1]: "Fi Zilal al-Quran," Vol. 7, p. 605.

Ayahs
