Commentary

What is Your Logical Argument, Really?!

These verses continue the previous argumentative discourse against those who deny the Quran, the prophethood of the Prophet صلى الله عليه وآله, and the power of the Lord.

These are verses that all begin with "أَمْ" (Or), which here serves as an "interrogative," forming a fascinating chain of reasoning consisting of "eleven consecutive questions" (in the form of rhetorical interrogation). To put it more clearly, it closes all avenues of escape for the opponents from every side, cornering them within these brief yet influential phrases in such a way that one involuntarily bows before its greatness and cohesion, offering admission and confession.

First, it begins with the issue of creation, saying: "Were they created by nothing? Or were they themselves the creators?" (أَمْ خُلِقُوا مِنْ غَيْرِ شَيْ‌ءٍ أَمْ هُمُ الْخالِقُونَ‌) [1]. This short and condensed phrase actually refers to the famous "Law of Causality" used in philosophy and theology to prove the existence of Allah. It posits that the world we live in is undoubtedly "hadith" (originated/contingent), because it is constantly changing, and whatever is in a state of change and transformation is subject to events (accidents), and that which is subject to events cannot possibly be eternal and primordial.

Now the question arises: if it is originated, it cannot be outside of five possibilities:

  1. It came into existence without a cause.
  2. It is its own cause.
  3. The effects within the world are the cause of its existence.
  4. This world is the effect of a cause which is itself the effect of another cause, and so on to infinity (infinite regress).
  5. This world is the creation of Allah, the Necessary Being (Wajib al-Wujud), whose existence stems from His own Pure Essence.

The falsehood of the first four possibilities is evident, because:

The existence of an effect without a cause is impossible; otherwise, everything should come into existence under any conditions, whereas this is not the case.

[^1]: Other possibilities have been suggested in the interpretation of this verse, including that the meaning is: "Were they created without a purpose, having no program or responsibility?" Although a group of commentators has chosen this meaning, in light of the second sentence (أَمْ هُمُ الْخالِقُونَ‌), it becomes clear that the intended meaning is what was stated above, i.e., "Were they created without a cause, or are they their own cause?"

The second possibility—that something brought itself into existence—is also impossible, because it implies that it existed before it existed, which is a contradiction (ponder this).

Similarly, the third possibility—that man's creations are the creator of man—is clearly false (as it necessitates a circular argument).

Likewise, the fourth possibility—the regression of causes and the extension of the chain of cause and effect to infinity—is also unacceptable. This is because an infinite number of effects and creatures are ultimately still creatures and require a Creator to bring them into being. Can an infinite number of zeros ever become a number? Or can light emerge from within infinite darkness? Or can self-sufficiency arise from infinite poverty and need?

Therefore, no path remains but to accept the fifth possibility: the creativity of the Necessary Being (ponder again).

And since the main pillar of this argument is the negation of the first and second possibilities, the Quran has contented itself with those. Now we see what profound reasoning is hidden within such a short phrase.

The next verse addresses another question regarding a claim at a lower level, saying: "Or did they create the heavens and the earth?" (أَمْ خَلَقُوا السَّماواتِ وَ الْأَرْضَ‌). If they did not come into being without a cause, and were not their own cause, are they then the Necessary Being and the Creator of the heavens and the earth? And if they are not the origin of the universe, has Allah delegated the matter of creating the heavens and the earth to them? And thus, are they creatures who themselves hold the command of creation?

Certainly, they can never make such a false claim; therefore, following this statement, it adds: "Nay, but they have no firm belief" (بَلْ لا يُوقِنُونَ‌). Yes, they are merely looking for an excuse to flee from faith.

And if they do not claim these things and have no share in the matter of creation, "Or are the treasures of your Lord with them?" (أَمْ عِنْدَهُمْ خَزائِنُ رَبِّكَ‌) [1], so that they may grant the "blessing of prophethood and knowledge" or other provisions to whomever they wish, and withhold them from whomever they wish? "Or are they the controllers [of the universe's affairs], having authority and dominance over all?" (أَمْ هُمُ الْمُصَيْطِرُونَ‌).

They can never claim to be the treasurers of the Lord, nor do they have dominance over the management of this world. Their weakness and helplessness in the face of an accident, a disease, or even a tiny insect, as well as their need for the most basic means of life, are the best evidence for negating these powers from them. It is only whims, ambition, selfishness, prejudice, and stubbornness that have led them to deny the truths.

"Musaytirun" (Controllers) refers to the "Lords of the Species" (Arbab al-Anwa'), which was part of the superstitions of the ancients.

[^1]: "Khaza'in" is the plural of "khazinah," meaning the source and center of anything gathered and stored for preservation and to prevent others from accessing it. The Holy Quran says: "And there is not a thing but that with Us are its depositories, and We do not send it down except according to a known measure" (Al-Hijr, 21).

They believed that every species in the world—including humans, animal species, plants, and others—had a specific manager and nurturer, which they called its "Lord of the Species," and they addressed God as the "Lord of Lords." This polytheistic belief is rejected from the Islamic perspective; in the verses of the Quran, the management of the entire universe is attributed to Allah, and we call Him "the Lord of the Worlds" (Rabb al-'Alamin).

This word is originally derived from "satr," which means the rows of words during writing. A "musaytir" is someone who has dominance over an affair and directs it, just as a writer has dominance over the lines of his own speech. (It should be noted that this word is written with both "Sad" and "Sin," and both have the same meaning, although the famous script of the Quran uses "Sad").

It is certain that neither the deniers of the Prophet's صلى الله عليه وآله mission and the polytheists of the Age of Ignorance, nor anyone else, claimed any of the five aforementioned matters. Therefore, in the next verse, it moves to another stage, saying: "Or have they a stairway [to heaven] by which they listen [to the revelation]?" (أَمْ لَهُمْ سُلَّمٌ يَسْتَمِعُونَ فِيهِ‌). Since they might claim knowledge of the secrets of heaven, the Quran immediately demands proof from them, saying: "Then let their listener bring a clear authority [proof]" (فَلْيَأْتِ مُسْتَمِعُهُمْ بِسُلْطانٍ مُبِينٍ‌).

Certainly, if they had such a claim, it would not exceed mere words, and they would never have any proof for it [1].

Then it adds: Is this unjust attribution they give to the angels, saying they are the daughters of Allah, acceptable? "Or has He daughters while you have sons?" (أَمْ لَهُ الْبَناتُ وَ لَكُمُ الْبَنُونَ‌). This points to the fact that one of their false beliefs and thoughts was an intense hatred for daughters; if they were informed that their wife had borne a daughter, their faces would turn black with grief and shame. Yet, despite this, they called the angels the daughters of Allah! If they are connected to the higher world and familiar with the secrets of revelation, is the sample of their revelation these ridiculous superstitions and these disgraceful, shameful beliefs?

It is obvious that daughters and sons do not differ in terms of human value; the expression in the above verse is actually a type of argument using the opponent's own false belief against them. The Quran emphasizes the negation of this superstitious belief in numerous verses, bringing them to trial and exposing them in this regard [2].

[^1]: "Sullam" means a ladder, and sometimes any kind of means. There is discussion among commentators as to what they claimed to be listening to; some interpreted it as revelation, others as the labels they gave the Prophet صلى الله عليه وآله like "poet" and "madman," or the partners they imagined for Allah, and some interpreted it as the negation of prophethood from the Prophet صلى الله عليه وآله (combining these meanings is not unlikely, though the first meaning is the clearest).

[^2]: Regarding why the pre-Islamic Arabs considered angels to be daughters of Allah while simultaneously hating and loathing female children, and the vivid arguments the Quran presents against them, we have had more extensive discussions in Volume 11, page 266 onwards (under verse 57 of Surah An-Nahl) and in Volume 19, page 170 onwards (under verse 149 of Surah As-Saffat).

Then, descending from this stage as well, it mentions another matter that could potentially be used as an excuse by them, saying: "Or do you ask them a payment, so they are burdened by a load of debt?" (أَمْ تَسْئَلُهُمْ أَجْراً فَهُمْ مِنْ مَغْرَمٍ مُثْقَلُونَ‌). "Maghram" is derived from "ghurm," meaning a loss that affects a person without cause, and "gharim" is applied to both the creditor and the debtor.

"Muthqal" is from "ithqal," meaning the imposition of hardship and a heavy burden. Therefore, the meaning of the sentence is: "Do you demand a penalty from them for conveying the message, which they are unable to pay and for this reason they do not believe?" This point is repeated many times in the Holy Quran—not only regarding the Prophet of Islam صلى الله عليه وآله but regarding many prophets—that one of the first statements of the prophets was: "We do not ask you for any reward for conveying the divine call," so that both their impartiality would be proven and no excuse would remain for the excuse-seekers.

Once again, it questions them, saying: "Or is the Unseen with them, so they write it down?" (أَمْ عِنْدَهُمُ الْغَيْبُ فَهُمْ يَكْتُبُونَ‌).

These people claim the Prophet is a poet for whose death and the disintegration of whose life they are waiting, and that with his death everything will end and his call will fall into oblivion (as mentioned a few verses prior from the polytheists: "We await for him some calamity of time").

How do they know they will be alive after the Prophet's passing? Who told them this secret of the Unseen?

There is also the possibility that the Quran is saying: If you claim to be aware of the secrets of the Unseen and know the divine laws and are independent of the Quran and the religion of Muhammad صلى الله عليه وآله, this is a great lie [1].

Then it proceeds to state another possibility, saying: "Or do they intend a plot? But those who disbelieve are themselves the object of the plot" (أَمْ يُرِيدُونَ كَيْداً فَالَّذِينَ كَفَرُوا هُمُ الْمَكِيدُونَ‌) [2]. According to this interpretation, the above verse is like verse 54 of Surah Al-Imran, which says:

[^1]: A group of commentators has interpreted "Ghayb" (Unseen) here as the "Preserved Tablet" (Lawh al-Mahfuz), and some have said it refers to claims made by some polytheists who said: "If there is a Resurrection, we will have a high status with Allah." However, these interpretations do not align well with the context of the above verses and their interrelation.

[^2]: "Kayd" is a type of stratagem that is sometimes used for good purposes, but is mostly used for evil. This word also means trickery, effort, and war.

"And they plotted, and Allah plotted. And Allah is the best of plotters."

This possibility has also been accepted by a group of commentators: that it means "their conspiracies will ultimately turn against themselves," similar to what appears in verse 43 of Surah Fatir: "And the evil plot does not encompass except its own people."

Combining both interpretations is also possible.

This verse can have another link with the previous verse: the enemies of Islam said, "We are waiting for the death of Muhammad." The Quran says it is one of two cases: either you claim he will die a natural death before you, which necessitates knowledge of the secrets of the Unseen; or if you mean he will be eliminated by your conspiracies, know that Allah's plans are superior to yours and your conspiracies will entrap yourselves.

And if you imagine that by gathering in "Dar al-Nadwa" and devising accusations such as "soothsaying," "madness," and "poetry" regarding the Prophet, you have the power to overcome him, you are mistaken. For the power of Allah is above all powers, and He has guaranteed the safety, salvation, and victory of His Prophet for the delivery of this global call!

And finally, in the last question, it asks them: Do they imagine they have a protector or helper? "Or have they a deity other than Allah?" (أَمْ لَهُمْ إِلهٌ غَيْرُ اللَّهِ‌). Then it adds: "Exalted is Allah above whatever they associate with Him" (سُبْحانَ اللَّهِ عَمَّا يُشْرِكُونَ‌).

Therefore, no one is capable of protecting them.

In this way, it places them before an extraordinary interrogation and a series of eleven chained questions, forcing them step by step to retreat and diminish their claims, then closing all escape routes and placing them in a complete deadlock.

How delightful are the arguments of the Quran and its method of questioning and interrogation; if there is a spirit of truth-seeking in someone, they will submit to it.

Interestingly, in the final verse, it mentions no specific proof for negating other deities, contenting itself only with the phrase "سُبْحانَ اللَّهِ عَمَّا يُشْرِكُونَ‌". This is because the falsehood of claiming divinity for idols made of stone and wood, or any other creature with their weaknesses and needs, is clearer than needing explanation or discussion. Furthermore, in other verses, arguments have been repeatedly made to invalidate this subject.

Ayahs

وَإِن يَرَوْا۟ كِسْفًا مِّنَ ٱلسَّمَآءِ سَاقِطًا يَقُولُوا۟ سَحَابٌ مَّرْكُومٌ (٤٤)
Were they to see a fragment falling from the sky, they would say, ‘A cumulous cloud.’ (44)
فَذَرْهُمْ حَتَّىٰ يُلَـٰقُوا۟ يَوْمَهُمُ ٱلَّذِى فِيهِ يُصْعَقُونَ (٤٥)
So leave them until they encounter the day when they will be thunderstruck, (45)
يَوْمَ لَا يُغْنِى عَنْهُمْ كَيْدُهُمْ شَيْـًٔا وَلَا هُمْ يُنصَرُونَ (٤٦)
the day when their guile will not avail them in any way, nor will they be helped. (46)
وَإِنَّ لِلَّذِينَ ظَلَمُوا۟ عَذَابًا دُونَ ذَٰلِكَ وَلَـٰكِنَّ أَكْثَرَهُمْ لَا يَعْلَمُونَ (٤٧)
Indeed there is a punishment besides that for those who do wrong, but most of them do not know. (47)
وَٱصْبِرْ لِحُكْمِ رَبِّكَ فَإِنَّكَ بِأَعْيُنِنَا ۖ وَسَبِّحْ بِحَمْدِ رَبِّكَ حِينَ تَقُومُ (٤٨)
So submit patiently to the judgement of your Lord, for indeed you fare before Our eyes. And celebrate the praise of your Lord when you rise [at dawn], (48)
وَمِنَ ٱلَّيْلِ فَسَبِّحْهُ وَإِدْبَـٰرَ ٱلنُّجُومِ (٤٩)
and also glorify Him during the night and at the receding of the stars. (49)

Commentary

You Are Under Our Full Protection

Following the discussion in the previous verses with the polytheists and stubborn deniers—a discussion that would clarify the truth for any seeker of reality—these verses pull back the curtain on their prejudice and obstinacy, stating: "They are so stubborn that if they were to see with their own eyes a fragment of the heavens falling as a divine punishment, they would say: 'You are mistaken; this is not a stone, it is but a mass of clouds falling to the earth!'" (وَ إِنْ يَرَوْا كِسْفاً مِنَ السَّماءِ ساقِطاً يَقُولُوا سحابٌ مَرْكُومٌ) [^1]. For those who are so obstinate that they deny sensory realities and interpret celestial stones as dense clouds—even though everyone has seen clouds when they are near the ground and knows they are nothing but a collection of vapor—how could this delicate vapor become so dense as to turn into stone?

The state of such individuals regarding spiritual truths is clear.

Indeed, the darkness of sin, whims of the soul, enmity, and stubbornness so cloud a person's horizon of vision that it eventually leads to the denial of the physical senses, and in such a state, there is no hope for their guidance.

"Markum" (مَرْكُومٌ) means "accumulated" or "dense," referring to something where parts are piled upon others.

Therefore, in the next verse, it adds: "Now that it is so, leave them alone and do not persist in guiding this stubborn group, until they meet their day of death and see with their own eyes the divine punishments awaiting them" (فَذَرْهُمْ حَتَّى يُلاقُوا يَوْمَهُمُ الَّذِي فِيهِ يُصْعَقُونَ). "Yus'aqun" (يُصْعَقُونَ) is derived from the root "sa'q" (صعق) and "is'aq" (اصعاق), meaning to cause death. Originally, it is taken from "sa'iqah" (صاعقة - lightning bolt/thunderclap), and since a lightning bolt destroys individuals, this word is also used to mean "to perish."

Some commentators have interpreted this phrase as the general death of humanity at the end of the world, which is the prelude to the Resurrection.

However, this interpretation seems unlikely because they will not remain until that time; rather, the first meaning is more apparent: leave them until the day of their death, which is the beginning of the punishments and retributions of the Hereafter.

From what we have said, it becomes clear that the phrase "dharhum" (leave them) is a threatening command, meaning to cease insisting on preaching to such unguidably stubborn individuals. Therefore, it neither contradicts the continuation of the Prophet's (صلى الله عليه وآله) general preaching nor the command for Jihad.

Thus, what some have claimed—that this verse was abrogated by the verses of Jihad—is in no way acceptable.

Then, describing that day, it says: "The day when their plotting and schemes shall avail them nothing, and all paths of escape shall be closed to them, and they shall not be helped from any side" (يَوْمَ لا يُغْنِي عَنْهُمْ كَيْدُهُمْ شَيْئاً وَ لا هُمْ يُنْصَرُونَ). Indeed, whoever dies, his "Minor Resurrection" begins (man mata qamat qiyamatuh), and it is a beginning for rewards and punishments—some of which are of a Purgatorial (Barzakh) nature, while another part will overtake them in the "Great Resurrection," the general assembly of humanity. In neither of these two stages will schemes be effective, nor will there be any helper against the Will of Allah.

Next, it adds: "Let them not imagine that only the punishment of Barzakh and the Resurrection are at play; rather, for those who have committed injustice and oppression, and practiced disbelief and polytheism, there is a punishment even before that in this very world, though most of them do not know" (وَ إِنَّ لِلَّذِينَ ظَلَمُوا عَذاباً دُونَ ذلِكَ وَ لكِنَّ أَكْثَرَهُمْ لا يَعْلَمُونَ). Yes, in this very world, they must expect punishments like those of previous nations, such as lightning bolts, earthquakes, celestial stones, drought, and famine, or being killed by the powerful hands of the warriors of the army of Monotheism—just as happened to a group of the leaders of polytheism in the Battle of Badr—unless they wake up, repent, and return to Allah.

Of course, a group of them were afflicted with famine and drought, and a group—as we mentioned—were killed in the Battle of Badr, but a large group also repented, believed, and joined the ranks of true Muslims, and Allah included them in His forgiveness [^1]. The phrase "but most of them do not know" (وَ لكِنَّ أَكْثَرَهُمْ لا يَعْلَمُونَ) indicates that they are generally unaware of the punishments awaiting them in this world and the next, implying that a minority of them are aware of this fact yet persist in their opposition due to stubbornness and enmity.

In the following verse, Allah invites the Prophet to patience and steadfastness in the face of all these obstructions, accusations, and insults, saying: "And be patient for the decision of your Lord" (وَ اصْبِرْ لِحُكْمِ رَبِّكَ) [^2]. If they call you a soothsayer, a madman, or a poet, be patient; if they consider the verses of the Quran to be fabrications attributed to Allah, be patient; and if they continue in their stubbornness and enmity despite all these logical proofs, show steadfastness.

[^1]: "Kisf" (on the weight of fisq) means a piece of anything, and given the expression "from the sky," it refers here to a celestial stone. Some lexicons suggest this word is the plural of kisfah (just as kisaf is also a plural), but most commentators have taken it as singular. The appearance of the verse under discussion suggests it is singular because its description is given in the singular form.

[^1]: Those who interpret the phrase "wherein they will swoon" as referring to the Day of Resurrection and the beginning of the Rising have taken the "punishment" in the verse under discussion to mean the punishment of the grave and Purgatorial retribution; however, since that interpretation is weak, this possibility is also weak.

[^2]: "The decision of your Lord" (hukm rabbika) may refer to the propagation of divine laws which the Prophet (صلى الله عليه وآله) is commanded to be patient in pursuing, or the punishment of Allah promised to the enemies (meaning: wait until the divine punishment overtakes them), or it may mean the commands and decrees of Allah (meaning: since Allah has commanded it, be patient). While a combination of all three interpretations is possible, the first interpretation seems most appropriate, especially considering the phrase "for you are before Our eyes."

Do not become discouraged, weak, or powerless.

"For you are before Our eyes [under Our watch] and you are in Our full protection" (فَإِنَّكَ بِأَعْيُنِنا).

We see everything, We are aware of everything, and We will not leave you alone.

The phrase "For you are before Our eyes" is a very delicate expression that signifies both the knowledge and awareness of the Lord and His full protection and grace. Indeed, when a person feels that a Great Being is watching and present, seeing all his efforts and struggles and protecting him against enemies, the realization of this gives him power and strength, as well as a greater sense of responsibility.

And since intimate prayer with Allah, His worship, and the glorification and sanctification of His Pure Essence grant a person peace and strength, following the command for patience, He says:

"And glorify the praise of your Lord when you arise" (وَ سَبِّحْ بِحَمْدِ رَبِّكَ حِينَ تَقُومُ).

When you arise in the dawn for worship and the Night Prayer. When you arise from sleep to perform the obligatory prayer. And when you arise from any gathering or assembly, praise and glorify Him.

Commentators have various interpretations of this verse, but a combination of all of them is possible: whether it is at dawn for the Night Prayer, after sleep for the obligatory prayer, or after rising from any gathering. Yes, illuminate and purify your soul and spirit with the glorification and praise of Allah, fragrance your tongue with His remembrance, seek help from His remembrance, and prepare yourself to combat the obstructions of the enemy! It is mentioned in several narrations that when the Prophet (صلى الله عليه وآله) arose from a gathering, he would perform the glorification and praise of Allah and say:

"It is the expiation of the gathering" (Innahu kaffaratul-majlis)! [^1]

Among these is a hadith stating that when the Prophet (صلى الله عليه وآله) arose from a gathering, he would say: "Glory be to Thee, O Allah, and with Thy praise, I bear witness that there is no god but Thee; I seek Thy forgiveness and turn to Thee in repentance." Some asked, "O Messenger of Allah, what are these words you say?" He replied: "These are words that Gabriel taught me; they are expiations for what occurs in gatherings." [^2]

Then, in the final verse, it adds: "And in the night also glorify Him, and at the retreat of the stars [at dawn]" (وَ مِنَ اللَّيْلِ فَسَبِّحْهُ وَ إِدْبارَ النُّجُومِ). Many commentators have interpreted the phrase "And in the night also glorify Him" as the "Night Prayer" (Salat al-Layl), and "the retreat of the stars" as the two units of the Sunnah prayer of Dawn (Nafilah of Fajr) which are performed at the onset of daybreak when the stars disappear in the light of morning.

In a hadith from Ali (عليه السلام), it is also stated that "the retreat of the stars" (idbar al-nujum) refers to the two units of the Dawn Sunnah prayer performed before the Fajr prayer at the setting of the stars. However, "after the prostrations" (adbar al-sujud) (which appears in Surah Qaf, verse 40) refers to the two units of Sunnah prayer performed after the Maghrib prayer (though the Maghrib Sunnah is four units, this hadith only mentions two of them). [^3]

[^1]: Tafsir al-Mizan, Vol. 19, p. 24. [^2]: Durr al-Manthur, Vol. 6, p. 120. [^3]: Majma' al-Bayan, under verse 40 of Surah Qaf (Vol. 9, p. 150).

In any case, worship, glorification, and praise of Allah in the heart of the night and at the onset of dawn have a different grace and purity; they are further from ostentation and hypocrisy, and the spiritual readiness for them is greater, as the distracting tasks of daily life are suspended. Nightly rest has granted the person peace, the noise and clamor have subsided, and it is effectively the same time the Prophet went on the Ascension (Mi'raj) and stood at the station of "two bows' length" in that private place of secrets, engaging in intimate prayer with his Lord.

For this reason, these two times are emphasized in the verses under discussion. And in a hadith from the Holy Prophet (صلى الله عليه وآله), we read: "The two units of Fajr [Sunnah] are better than the world and all that is in it." [^1]

O Allah! Grant us the success of rising at dawn and engaging in intimate prayer with Thee throughout our lives. O Lord! Make our hearts tranquil with Thy love, illuminated by Thy affection, and hopeful of Thy grace. O God! Grant us patience, endurance, steadfastness, and resilience against diabolical forces and the obstructions of Thy enemies, so that we may follow Thy Prophet, live by his Sunnah, and die upon his Sunnah.

Amen, O Lord of the Worlds. End of Surah At-Tur, 22 Safar 1406.
